section.title: Teach Me Your Way, O Lord
section.sectionRationale: The only psalm of David set among the Korah and Asaph collections of Book III, this personal prayer of a poor and needy servant appeals to God's mercy, uniqueness, and steadfast love, asking for an undivided heart, a request answered fully in the perfect, single-hearted devotion of Christ.
section.thesis: David, poor and needy yet trusting, appeals to the LORD's kindness, forgiveness, and steadfast love, asking to be taught God's way and given an undivided heart, a prayer answered in Christ, who both perfectly walked in the Father's way and unites divided hearts to Himself.
section.frameNote: Five movements: a personal plea from need, verses 1-5; confidence in God's readiness to answer, verses 6-7; praise for God's incomparable greatness, verses 8-10; a prayer for an undivided heart, verses 11-13; and a cry for help against enemies, verses 14-17.

verses.text: Incline Your ear, O LORD, and answer me, for I am poor and needy.
Preserve my soul, for I am godly. You are my God; save Your servant who trusts in You.
Be merciful to me, O Lord, for I call to You all day long.
Bring joy to Your servant, for to You, O Lord, I lift up my soul.
For You, O Lord, are kind and forgiving, rich in {{chesed|loving devotion}} to all who call on You.

units.label: Confidence That God Answers
units.note: trust in God's readiness to hear

verses.text: Hear my prayer, O LORD, and attend to my plea for mercy.
In the day of my distress I call on You, because You answer me.
O Lord, there is none like You among the gods, nor any works like Yours.
All the nations You have made will come and bow before You, O Lord, and they will glorify Your name.
For You are great and perform wonders; You alone are God.
Teach me Your way, O LORD, that I may walk in Your truth. Give me an {{yachad|undivided}} heart, that I may fear Your name.
I will praise You, O Lord my God, with all my heart; I will glorify Your name forever.
For great is Your loving devotion to me; You have delivered me from the depths of {{sheol|Sheol}}.
The arrogant rise against me, O God; a band of ruthless men seeks my life; they have no regard for You.
But You, O Lord, are a {{rachum|compassionate}} and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion and faithfulness.
Turn to me and have mercy; grant Your strength to Your servant; save the son of Your maidservant.
Show me a sign of Your goodness, that my enemies may see and be ashamed; for You, O LORD, have helped me and comforted me.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant love. David grounds his confidence in prayer not on his own merit but on the character of a God who is rich in this loyal love toward all who call on Him.

yachad.gloss: United, joined together, as one. David's prayer for an undivided heart asks that every competing loyalty be joined into a single, wholehearted devotion to God's name.

sheol.gloss: The realm of the dead, the grave. David praises God for a deliverance so great that it reached even to the brink of death itself.

rachum.gloss: Compassionate, full of tender mercy. This word, drawn from God's self-revelation to Moses, describes the deep, parental mercy David appeals to when facing ruthless enemies.

basic.context: David, poor and needy and hunted by ruthless enemies, prays this deeply personal psalm. He appeals again and again to God's mercy and steadfast love, praises God's incomparable greatness among all the nations, and asks for a heart that is wholly, undividedly devoted to God alone.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes David's own crisis, yet its pattern of humble appeal, praise for God's uniqueness, and prayer for an undivided heart offers a lasting model for anyone approaching God from a place of need.
basic.originalLanguages: David appeals to God's chesed, steadfast love, verse 5, and asks for a yachad, undivided or united, heart, verse 11. He praises the rachum, compassionate, God who has delivered him from the depths of sheol, the realm of the dead, verse 13.
basic.typology: David's plea for an undivided heart wholly devoted to God's name anticipates the perfectly single-hearted obedience of Christ, who always did what was pleasing to the Father, and who unites divided, wandering hearts to Himself.
basic.christ: David's deliverance from the depths of Sheol, verse 13, foreshadows the greater deliverance from death accomplished in Christ's resurrection, Acts 2:24-31. And the nations bowing before the LORD, verse 9, anticipates every tribe and tongue worshiping before the Lamb, Revelation 15:4.
basic.god: God is shown as kind, forgiving, rich in loving devotion, incomparably great among all so-called gods, compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, and utterly faithful, a God who answers in the day of distress.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 15:4 echoes the nations coming to worship the LORD. Acts 2:24-31 shows resurrection life triumphing over Sheol, ultimately in Christ. Philippians 2:9-11 pictures every knee bowing to Jesus, fulfilling verse 9's vision.
basic.oneStory: David's plea for an undivided heart and his confidence in the God who alone is God, who forgives, delivers from death, and answers prayer, finds its fullest answer in Christ, whose single-hearted obedience to the Father secures for us the very undivided devotion David longed for.

context.literary: A prayer of David, structured around repeated pleas for mercy interwoven with declarations of God's character, moving from personal need, verses 1-7, to praise of God's uniqueness, verses 8-10, to a prayer for wholehearted devotion, verses 11-13, and a final cry against enemies, verses 14-17.
context.historical: The absence of any reference to the temple suggests a setting before Solomon's reign, likely during one of the many seasons when David, though anointed king, was hunted by enemies at home or abroad.
context.canonical: Standing alone as a Davidic psalm within the Korahite and Asaphite psalms of Book III, this prayer models how the individual believer's cry for mercy fits within the larger story of God's covenant faithfulness to His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice a personal, urgent appeal for mercy grounded in God's proven character, and to model praying for singleness of heart even while surrounded by real and ruthless opposition.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific historical distress; it prescribes an abiding pattern of appealing to God's character rather than one's own merit, and praying for an undivided heart as an ongoing need, not a one-time achievement.

debates.question: Does verse 9's picture of all nations bowing before the LORD describe a hoped-for historical event or a final, eschatological reality?

views.label: It anticipates the historical spread of the knowledge of God through Israel's witness
views.case: Old Testament hope regularly pictured surrounding nations coming to acknowledge Israel's God as His people were faithful, a hope partially realized whenever Gentiles turned to worship the LORD.

views.label: It points forward to the final, universal worship pictured in Revelation
views.case: The sweeping, unconditional language, 'all the nations,' exceeds any single historical fulfillment and finds its full realization only in the final gathering of every nation before God's throne, Revelation 15:4.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that God's plan always included the nations, a plan David glimpsed and that reaches its climax in the worldwide worship of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: David appeals to God's chesed, steadfast covenant love, verse 5, and prays for a yachad, undivided, heart, verse 11, so that his devotion to God's name would have no rival. He praises the rachum, compassionate, God who has rescued him from the depths of sheol, verse 13, and who alone is God among all the nations, verse 10.

typology.type: An undivided heart wholly devoted to God's name, verse 11
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's perfect, single-hearted obedience to the Father, who always did what pleased Him, John 8:29, and who unites His people's divided hearts to Himself.

typology.type: The nations bowing and glorifying God's name, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled as every nation comes to worship before the Lamb, Revelation 15:4, and every knee bows to Jesus, Philippians 2:10-11.

study.christ: David's deliverance from the depths of Sheol, verse 13, foreshadows the resurrection triumph over death accomplished fully in Christ, whom God raised up, having loosed the pains of death, Acts 2:24. David's prayer for an undivided heart, verse 11, finds its perfect answer in Jesus, whose heart was never divided in its devotion to the Father, and who, by His Spirit, unites the wandering hearts of His people to Himself.
study.god: God is kind and forgiving, rich in steadfast love to all who call on Him, incomparably great among all so-called gods, compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion and faithfulness, and reliably present to answer in the day of distress.

crossReferences.note: All nations will come and worship before God, fulfilling the vision of verse 9.
Christ's resurrection triumphs fully over Sheol, the deliverance David glimpses in verse 13.
Every knee bows and every tongue confesses Jesus as Lord, the ultimate fulfillment of the nations glorifying God's name.

study.oneStory: David's personal cry for mercy, his praise of the one true God among the nations, and his prayer for an undivided heart together anticipate Christ, whose perfectly undivided devotion to the Father and whose victory over Sheol answer this psalm's deepest longings and draw the nations to worship.

sermon.title: The Lord Is Great and Does Wondrous Things
sermon.why: Preaching directly from Psalm 86:8-10, Piper draws out the psalm's confession that the LORD alone is God among all the nations, grounding worship and prayer in God's incomparable greatness, the very heart of this psalm's praise.

quiz.q: How does David describe himself as he begins his prayer in verses 1-2?

options.text: Poor and needy, yet godly and trusting in God.
options.note: David's opening plea rests on his neediness and his trust, not on his own strength.

options.text: Wealthy and self-sufficient.
options.note: David describes himself as poor and needy, the opposite of self-sufficient.

options.text: A king with no enemies.
options.note: The psalm later reveals ruthless enemies seeking his life; he is not without opposition.

options.text: A servant who trusts in God.
options.note: This is true, verse 2, and closely related, though the fuller self-description in verses 1-2 is poor, needy, and godly.

quiz.q: What does David declare about God in verse 10?

options.text: You are great and perform wonders; You alone are God.
options.note: Verse 10 is the psalm's clear declaration of God's sole and supreme divinity.

options.text: There are many gods equal to the LORD.
options.note: The psalm insists there is none like the LORD and that He alone is God.

options.text: God is distant and uninvolved.
options.note: The psalm portrays an involved God who answers prayer and performs wonders.

options.text: All the nations will one day bow and glorify His name.
options.note: This truth appears in verse 9, closely related, though verse 10 specifically declares God's sole and supreme deity: You alone are God.

quiz.q: What does David ask for in verse 11?

options.text: To be taught God's way and given an undivided heart to fear His name.
options.note: Verse 11 is David's central prayer for wholehearted, singular devotion to God.

options.text: To be given more wealth and possessions.
options.note: David's request is for spiritual singleness of heart, not material gain.

options.text: To be freed from ever praying again.
options.note: The psalm models ongoing prayer, not its cessation.

options.text: To have his soul preserved as God's servant.
options.note: This is true and part of David's earlier plea, verse 2, though verse 11 specifically prays for being taught God's way and given an undivided heart.

quiz.q: What deliverance does David celebrate in verse 13?

options.text: Being delivered from the depths of Sheol because of God's great loving devotion.
options.note: Verse 13 praises God's steadfast love for a deliverance reaching even to the brink of death.

options.text: Victory in a specific military battle.
options.note: The verse speaks of deliverance from Sheol, not a named battle.

options.text: Being rescued from a life-threatening danger.
options.note: This is a fair general description, though the verse specifically names deliverance from the depths of Sheol, the realm of the dead.

options.text: Nothing; the verse describes ongoing suffering.
options.note: The verse is a clear declaration of past deliverance, not ongoing suffering.

quiz.q: How does David's prayer for an undivided heart point toward Christ?

options.text: It anticipates Christ's perfectly single-hearted devotion to the Father, who unites divided hearts to Himself.
options.note: Jesus always did what pleased the Father, John 8:29, and He alone can give His people the undivided heart David asked for.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The theme of wholehearted devotion runs directly into Christ's own perfect obedience.

options.text: It predicts a specific future king of Israel unrelated to Jesus.
options.note: The psalm's language of undivided devotion to God finds its fulfillment in Christ, not merely a future earthly king.

options.text: It shows the deep human longing for wholehearted devotion to God.
options.note: This is true as far as it goes, but the prayer's answer is found not merely in longing but in Christ's own perfect obedience and His gift of a new heart to His people.

questions: David repeatedly appeals to God's character rather than his own merit. When you pray, are you tempted to rely on your own worthiness rather than on God's steadfast love?
Verse 11 prays for an undivided heart. What competing loyalties or divided affections most threaten your wholehearted devotion to God right now?
David praises God's incomparable greatness even while surrounded by ruthless enemies. How does remembering who God is change the way you face opposition or hardship?
David's deliverance from Sheol points forward to Christ's resurrection victory over death. How does the assurance of that greater deliverance shape your hope today?
Verse 17 asks for a visible sign of God's goodness so that enemies would be ashamed. Where in your life could you ask God for evidence of His goodness that would strengthen your witness to others?

translationNotes.note: Godly renders a Hebrew word meaning one who is faithful or the recipient of God's covenant loyalty, not a claim of sinless perfection.
Undivided heart translates a phrase meaning literally a heart made one, picturing every scattered loyalty gathered into a single devotion to God.
Son of Your maidservant is a humble self-description meaning one born into a household devoted to serving God from birth.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB (Berean Standard Bible), cross-checked against the section map bounds (Psalm 86:1-17, 17 verses). Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's message 'The Lord Is Great and Does Wondrous Things' (Psalm 86:8-10) at desiringgod.org.
